content.text: Python's exception mechanism gives agent systems a structured, unambiguous way to signal failure. When a tool call times out, a model response cannot be parsed, or an external API returns an error, exceptions propagate that failure up the call stack with rich diagnostic context — making it possible for a central orchestration loop to decide whether to retry, fall back, or abort rather than silently producing wrong results.
is a Python object — typically a subclass of
— that interrupts normal control flow when
is executed. Python immediately unwinds the call stack, skipping ordinary code in each frame, until it finds a matching
clause. If none is found, the interpreter prints a traceback and terminates the process.
Every exception carries at minimum a human-readable message accessible via
and a machine-readable type accessible via
. More specific subclasses add structured attributes — for example, an HTTP client might add a
field — so handlers can branch on details without parsing the message string.
Python organises exceptions in a tree rooted at
. Knowing the tree prevents accidental over- or under-catching:
— root of everything; avoid catching unless you own the top-level process boundary
— base of all application and library errors; catch this at agent boundaries to avoid swallowing process signals
— invalid argument values; common when parsing model output
— wrong type; often from mismatched tool signatures
— missing keys or indices in parsed JSON
— network and I/O failures in API calls
; raised when model text is not valid JSON
in Python 3.8+; must not be swallowed by generic

Agents operate at the intersection of multiple unreliable systems: LLM APIs with rate limits and occasional malformed responses, external tools that depend on network connectivity, sandboxed code execution with resource limits, and parsing logic that must tolerate model hallucinations. Any one of these can fail on any invocation.
Without exceptions, the only alternatives are return values that encode failure: sentinels like

, error dicts like {"error": "timeout"}, or boolean flags. Sentinel-based approaches require every caller to inspect the return value and propagate failure manually. One missed check silently drops the error and passes corrupted state to the next step. Exceptions make failure impossible to ignore: unless a handler explicitly catches and suppresses an exception, it propagates automatically, even across async boundaries.

For an agent loop that may run hundreds of tool calls per session, silent failures compound quickly. An exception that surfaces a parse error on the third tool call prevents the agent from acting on garbage data and lets the orchestrator log, retry, or present a graceful error — behaviour that is only possible when failure is explicit and typed.
— distinguish rate-limit errors from parse errors from timeouts with a single isinstance check, enabling different recovery strategies for each
— exceptions travel up the call stack without any intermediate code needing to pass them along; the orchestrator receives failures from deeply nested tool calls automatically
— attach structured fields (retry_after, status_code, tool_name) to custom exception classes so handlers have everything needed for recovery decisions
preserves the original traceback while adding semantic context, making post-hoc debugging faster
— tool functions raise; orchestration code catches and converts to recovery actions; these responsibilities do not bleed into each other
block runs regardless of whether an exception occurred, enabling deterministic cleanup of HTTP connections, file handles, and spans

— exceptions can jump across many stack frames, making the flow of execution harder to follow than explicit error returns; heavy use in library code requires clear documentation of what each function can raise
at the top of the agent loop prevents crashes but also hides programming errors (
) that should be fixed, not swallowed
— different LLM SDKs raise different exception hierarchies; wrapping them adds boilerplate but is necessary for portable agent code
— raising and catching exceptions involves stack unwinding and object allocation; negligible for rare agent failures but relevant if exceptions are used for normal per-token control flow
must not be caught by generic handlers; structured concurrency frameworks (anyio, trio) introduce additional exception groups that require special handling in Python 3.11+
and records the current execution frame as the traceback start. The interpreter then exits the current frame, moves up to the caller, and checks whether that frame has an active
or any subclass. The first matching clause wins; subsequent clauses in the same
block are skipped. If no clause matches, Python continues unwinding upward until it reaches the top-level frame, where it prints the traceback and exits.
The try / except / else / finally anatomy
When wrapping a low-level error in a domain-specific exception, always use
to the original exception and tells Python to display both tracebacks when printing. Omitting
instead, which still shows the chained traceback but with the message "during handling of the above exception" — slightly less explicit. Use
only when the original exception is irrelevant noise that would confuse readers.

The diagram below shows how exceptions flow through a typical agentic system: tool functions raise typed exceptions at the boundary where failures are detected, the exceptions propagate up to a central handler in the orchestration loop, and the handler routes each type to a recovery path — retry, fallback, or abort.

attrs.alt: Exception handling flow in an agentic system showing how tool errors propagate to the orchestrator and are routed to recovery actions

content.text: Three layers carry distinct responsibilities. Tool functions detect and raise specific exceptions at the point of failure, enriching them with domain context. The dispatcher wraps tool invocations, translating SDK-specific exceptions into the agent's own exception hierarchy. The orchestrator loop holds the catch clauses that decide what recovery action to take — no other layer makes that decision.

Raise an exception when a function cannot satisfy its contract and the caller must be aware. The key test: if the caller would produce wrong results by continuing with a missing or invalid return value, raise rather than return a sentinel.
when a tool invocation fails at the network, parsing, or validation layer — these are genuine errors the orchestrator must handle
when a required argument or configuration is absent or invalid — fail fast at startup rather than producing subtle bugs later
at the model boundary when the LLM itself must read and reason about the failure — some frameworks convert tool errors to message-role entries the model sees directly
for expected, per-request variation — an empty search result is not an error; return an empty list
when the condition is expected and the caller must handle both paths
in generic handlers — these are process-level signals that must propagate to the runtime

A well-structured implementation defines a custom exception hierarchy, wraps external SDK calls at a single boundary, and lets the orchestration loop hold the recovery logic. The following example shows the complete pattern for a tool-calling agent.
Step 2 — Wrap SDK calls at the boundary
Example 1 — Custom exception with structured fields
Example 2 — Retry loop with exponential backoff

"""Runnable example: retry with backoff using exception types."""

import random, time

class RateLimitError(Exception):
    def __init__(self, retry_after: float):
        super().__init__(f"rate limited, retry after {retry_after}s")
        self.retry_after = retry_after

class FatalAPIError(Exception):
    pass

# Simulate an API that rate-limits the first two calls
_call_count = 0

def flaky_api_call() -> str:
    global _call_count
    _call_count += 1
    if _call_count < 3:
        raise RateLimitError(retry_after=0.05)  # 50ms for demo
    return "success"

def call_with_retry(max_attempts: int = 5) -> str:
    for attempt in range(1, max_attempts + 1):
        try:
            result = flaky_api_call()
            print(f"Attempt {attempt}: succeeded")
            return result
        except RateLimitError as exc:
            if attempt == max_attempts:
                raise
            wait = exc.retry_after
            print(f"Attempt {attempt}: rate limited, waiting {wait}s")
            time.sleep(wait)
        except FatalAPIError:
            print(f"Attempt {attempt}: fatal error, aborting")
            raise
    raise RuntimeError("exceeded max retries")  # should not reach here

result = call_with_retry()
print(f"Final result: {result}")
# Output:
# Attempt 1: rate limited, waiting 0.05s
# Attempt 2: rate limited, waiting 0.05s
# Attempt 3: succeeded
# Final result: success
Example 3 — JavaScript: exception patterns in a Node.js agent

) maps HTTP response codes to typed exception classes. An agent built on top of it uses those types in its orchestrator to decide whether to wait and retry, abort the turn, or surface an error to the user.

import anthropic
import time
import logging

logger = logging.getLogger(__name__)
client = anthropic.Anthropic()

def run_turn(messages: list[dict], max_retries: int = 3) -> str:
    for attempt in range(1, max_retries + 1):
        try:
            response = client.messages.create(
                model="claude-opus-4-5",
                max_tokens=1024,
                messages=messages,
            )
            return response.content[0].text

        except anthropic.RateLimitError as exc:
            # 429 — back off and retry
            if attempt == max_retries:
                raise
            wait = 2 ** attempt
            logger.warning("rate limited (attempt %d), waiting %ds", attempt, wait)
            time.sleep(wait)

        except anthropic.InternalServerError as exc:
            # 5xx — transient; retry
            if attempt == max_retries:
                raise
            logger.warning("server error %s (attempt %d)", exc.status_code, attempt)
            time.sleep(1)

        except anthropic.BadRequestError as exc:
            # 400 — our payload is wrong; do not retry
            logger.error("bad request: %s", exc.message)
            raise  # propagate immediately

        except anthropic.AuthenticationError:
            # 401 — missing or invalid API key
            raise RuntimeError("Invalid API key — check ANTHROPIC_API_KEY") from None
The Anthropic SDK hierarchy mirrors Python's conventions: all SDK errors subclass
. The agent's handler does not need to know SDK internals — it only branches on the types that require different recovery strategies.
Silent swallow — the most dangerous pattern
Catching too broadly at the tool level
inside a tool function hides errors that the orchestrator should see; only convert specific expected errors
— in asyncio, exceptions raised inside tasks that are never awaited are silently discarded; always attach done callbacks or use task groups
Define one exception hierarchy per library or agent package
— root your custom errors in a single base class; callers can catch the base to handle everything or subclasses for specific recovery paths
Raise at the point of detection, catch at the point of recovery
— tool functions raise; the orchestrator catches; intermediate layers pass exceptions through transparently
Always use raise ... from when wrapping
— preserves the original traceback for debugging without exposing internal SDK details to higher layers
Log at the catch site, not at the raise site
— the raise site does not know whether the caller will log or retry; the catch site knows the context and can log at the appropriate level
Use finally for cleanup, not just success paths
— HTTP client spans, database transactions, and file handles must close even when exceptions occur
Distinguish transient from fatal errors in the type hierarchy
base class lets the retry loop match all retryable errors with a single isinstance check
Test the failure paths as rigorously as the happy path
with context managers to assert both the exception type and its attributes

Raising an exception in CPython involves allocating an exception object, capturing the current stack frame, and unwinding each intermediate frame. Benchmarks show raising and catching a simple exception costs roughly 5–20 µs — negligible for agent systems where individual LLM API calls take 200 ms to 10 s. Exception overhead only becomes relevant if exceptions are used as normal per-iteration control flow inside tight loops (millions of iterations per second).

For agent systems specifically, the main scalability concern is not exception performance but retry amplification: if every transient failure triggers an immediate retry, a partial outage can turn a brief API hiccup into a cascading overload. The solution is structured retry with exponential backoff and jitter, bounded by both a maximum number of retries and a maximum total elapsed time.
In highly concurrent agent systems running many parallel tool calls, exception-driven retry can interact poorly with asyncio cancellation: a cancelled task raises
equivalent. The remedy is to catch transient errors and
provides declarative retry logic built on exception types, removing the retry loop boilerplate from agent code.

import pytest
from errors import ToolParseError

def test_parse_error_raised_on_empty_output():
    with pytest.raises(ToolParseError, match="empty"):
        parse_tool_call("")   # should raise ToolParseError

def test_parse_error_chains_cause():
    with pytest.raises(ToolParseError) as exc_info:
        parse_tool_call("{bad json}")
    assert isinstance(exc_info.value.__cause__, ValueError)
call logs the message and appends the full traceback automatically. With
emits structured JSON including the traceback, tool name, and any extra bound context — essential for correlating failures across distributed agent runs.
— automatic exception capture with full context; integrates with asyncio and popular frameworks
— records exception events on spans, enabling distributed tracing across tool calls
— raises structured validation errors with field-level detail when tool arguments or model outputs fail schema validation

Several patterns compete with exceptions for error signaling. The right choice depends on whether errors are expected, how many callers need to handle them, and whether the language has built-in support for the pattern.
Unexpected failures that require recovery by callers
Avoid in agent code; only for trivially optional returns
Auditing side-channel; never a substitute for signaling

Exception handling has direct implications for agent reliability and security. An over-broad catch that swallows all exceptions can cause the agent to continue executing after a security boundary has been violated — for example, catching and ignoring a
that should have prevented a file write. Similarly, swallowing exceptions from input validation lets malformed tool arguments proceed to execution.
Several reliability patterns deserve explicit implementation in production agent systems:
— if the catch-all handler does not recognise the exception type, abort the current turn and log the full traceback rather than attempting to continue
Sanitise exception messages before surfacing to users
— tracebacks and internal exception messages may contain API keys, file paths, or internal architecture details; convert to user-facing messages at the boundary
Treat exception handling as part of the threat model
— an adversarial prompt could cause a tool to raise an exception that carries sensitive data in its message; log-only handlers that write to shared logs are a data exfiltration vector
— a downstream API that returns 503 on every call should not trigger infinite retries; implement a circuit breaker that opens after a threshold of consecutive failures
Record exception metadata in audit logs
— exception type, tool name, and sanitised message provide the minimum information needed to diagnose and replay failures post-hoc

Think of exceptions as fire alarms: when something goes wrong inside a building (function), the alarm fires, all normal activities stop, and everyone follows the evacuation route (stack unwinding) to the assembly point (the nearest matching catch clause). The alarm carries information about where it fired (traceback) and why (exception message). Uncaught alarms shut the building down (process exit). Catching an alarm and passing (doing nothing) is like unplugging the detector — the building stays open but nobody knows it might be on fire.

Applied to agentic systems: each tool invocation is a room in the building. The orchestration loop is the safety warden at the assembly point. The warden's job is to read the alarm label (exception type), consult the evacuation plan (recovery policy), and decide whether to send a firefighter (retry), evacuate the floor (abort the turn), or accept a controlled burn (return an error to the model).
Exceptions are Python's primary mechanism for signalling failure with type, message, and traceback — use them for all tool, network, and parsing failures in agent code
Define a custom exception hierarchy with a single
base class and subclasses that distinguish transient (retryable) from fatal (non-retryable) failures
when wrapping lower-level errors — this chains the cause and preserves both tracebacks for debugging
Catch specific exception types in the orchestration loop; avoid catching
Log at the catch site with
to capture the full traceback automatically; do not log at the raise site
Implement exponential backoff with jitter for transient errors and circuit breaking for sustained outages to prevent retry amplification under load
Sanitise exception messages before surfacing to end users — internal details, file paths, and API keys must not reach user-facing error strings
to test both the exception type and its structured fields; failure paths deserve the same coverage as the happy path
